name: Frontend interview prep (Nagarro)
description: The 20-topic Nagarro/Tesco frontend prep doc in full: JS internals (Map/WeakMap, generators, closures, event loop), React (hooks, refs, Redux Toolkit), CSS/layout, a11y, security, Web Vitals, microfrontends and TypeScript.

topics.name: Object vs Map
topics.description: Choosing between plain objects and Map for keyed collections; key coercion, iteration, size.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

JavaScript gives you two ways to store key–value pairs: the plain Object ({}) and the Map class. They look similar but behave differently, and interviews often ask when to use which.

## What problem does it solve?

Objects were designed for structured records, not as general dictionaries. When you use one as a dictionary, you hit surprises — Map was added to fix exactly those.

## How it works

The big differences:
- Key types: object keys are silently converted to strings. If you use an object as a key, it becomes the string "[object Object]" — so two different object keys collide and overwrite each other. Map keeps any key type as-is: objects, functions, numbers.
- Order and size: Map remembers insertion order and has a fast .size property. For objects you must count Object.keys().
- Inherited baggage: a plain object inherits methods like toString. If a key happens to be named "toString", it overwrites the method. Map has no such baggage — it is a clean container.
- Performance: Map is faster when you add and delete keys a lot.
- JSON: objects serialize to JSON directly; Maps need manual conversion.

Rule of thumb: simple fixed-shape data (a config, a record) → Object. A real dictionary, non-string keys, or frequent add/remove → Map.

WeakMap is a special Map whose keys are held "weakly": if nothing else references a key object, the garbage collector may free it — and its cache entry disappears with it. That makes WeakMap perfect for caching computed data per object without causing memory leaks. The price: you cannot list its contents or ask its size.

## Key terms

- Key coercion — object keys become strings, causing collisions
- Insertion order — Map iterates in the order items were added
- WeakMap — object-keyed cache that lets the GC clean up
- Garbage collector (GC) — frees memory nothing points to anymore

topics.name: Event delegation
topics.description: Capturing vs bubbling, delegating handlers to a common ancestor.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

Event delegation means putting ONE event listener on a parent element instead of a separate listener on every child, and figuring out which child was clicked from the event object.

## What problem does it solve?

A list with 1,000 items would need 1,000 click listeners — wasteful, and items added later would have none. One listener on the list handles all of them, including future ones.

## How it works

It works because of how events travel in the DOM. When you click a button, the event moves in three phases:
- 1. Capturing: from the window DOWN through every ancestor to the target.
- 2. Target: the element actually clicked.
- 3. Bubbling: back UP from the target to the window.

Listeners run in the bubbling phase by default — so a listener on the parent hears clicks on its children. Inside the handler, event.target is the element that was actually clicked; use event.target.closest("li") to find the list item it belongs to (the click may land on a span inside it).

Things people mix up:
- event.target (what was clicked) vs event.currentTarget (where the listener is attached).
- stopPropagation() stops the event from traveling further; preventDefault() stops the browser's default action (following a link, submitting a form). They are independent.
- stopImmediatePropagation() also cancels other listeners on the SAME element.

Watch out: some events do not bubble — focus/blur and mouseenter/mouseleave. Use their bubbling twins focusin/focusout and mouseover/mouseout for delegation.

## Key terms

- Bubbling — events travel up through ancestors; delegation relies on it
- event.target.closest(selector) — safely find which item was clicked
- preventDefault vs stopPropagation — cancel the action vs stop the travel
- focusin/focusout — the bubbling versions of focus/blur

topics.name: WebSocket vs SSE
topics.description: Bidirectional WebSocket vs one-way server-sent events vs polling for realtime UI.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

Two technologies for pushing live data from a server to a web page without the page asking again and again: WebSocket (a two-way connection) and Server-Sent Events / SSE (a one-way stream from server to browser).

## What problem does it solve?

Normal HTTP is request–response: the client asks, the server answers, done. But a chat app or a live dashboard needs the SERVER to send data whenever something happens. The crude workaround is polling — asking every few seconds — which wastes requests. Long polling (the server holds the request open until there is news) is better but still clunky. WebSocket and SSE keep one connection open instead.

## How it works

WebSocket: starts as an HTTP request that "upgrades" into a different protocol (ws:// or encrypted wss://). After that, both sides can send messages at any time — full two-way traffic. Costs: if the connection drops, YOU must write the reconnect logic; proxies and firewalls sometimes dislike it.

SSE: stays plain HTTP. The server keeps the response open and streams text events; the browser's EventSource object receives them. Reconnection is automatic and built in. Limits: one-way only (server → client) and text only.

How to choose: does the CLIENT also need to send a continuous stream (chat messages, game moves, live cursors)? → WebSocket. Does only the server push (notifications, price tickers, dashboards)? → SSE is simpler and more robust.

## Key terms

- Polling / long polling — repeatedly asking; the pre-streaming workarounds
- Upgrade handshake — how an HTTP request becomes a WebSocket
- EventSource — the browser API for SSE, with automatic reconnect
- Full-duplex — both directions at once (WebSocket)

topics.name: CSS box-sizing
topics.description: content-box vs border-box and how padding/border affect the box model.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

Every element on a page is a rectangular box with four layers, inside out: content → padding → border → margin. The CSS property box-sizing decides what the width you set actually refers to.

## What problem does it solve?

You write width: 200px, add padding: 20px and a 2px border — and the element is suddenly 244px wide, breaking your layout. That is the default model surprising you.

## How it works

- content-box (the default): width applies to the CONTENT only. Padding and border are added ON TOP. 200 + 2×20 + 2×2 = 244px total.
- border-box: width applies to the whole visible box (content + padding + border). You say 200px, you get 200px; the content area shrinks to make room.

border-box matches how humans think, which is why virtually every project overrides the default with this reset:

*, *::before, *::after { box-sizing: border-box; }

With border-box, width: 50% really means half the parent — padding no longer causes overflow. All the big CSS frameworks (Tailwind, Bootstrap) ship this reset.

Fun history: old Internet Explorer used border-box "incorrectly" while the standard said content-box — and IE's version turned out to be the sensible one.

## Key terms

- Box model — content, padding, border, margin layers
- content-box — width = content only (the default)
- border-box — width = the whole visible box (what you want)
- Universal reset — the *, *::before, *::after rule applying border-box everywhere

topics.name: Web accessibility (a11y)
topics.description: Semantic HTML, ARIA, keyboard navigation, focus management, contrast.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

Accessibility (a11y) means building pages that everyone can use — including people who rely on screen readers, keyboards instead of a mouse, or high-contrast text. WCAG is the standard that defines the requirements, with levels A / AA (the common target) / AAA.

## What problem does it solve?

A significant share of users cannot use a mouse, cannot see low-contrast text, or navigate by having the page read aloud. Inaccessible pages simply lock them out — and in the EU and US, accessibility is increasingly a legal requirement, not a courtesy.

## How it works

The foundation is semantic HTML: use the element that means what you mean. header, nav, main, footer give screen-reader users landmarks to jump between. A real button is focusable and works with Enter/Space automatically; a div with onclick is invisible to the keyboard. The golden rule: "No ARIA is better than bad ARIA" — reach for ARIA attributes only when no native element can express it (aria-label for icon-only buttons, aria-live for announcing dynamic updates, role="dialog" for modals).

The keyboard checklist:
- Everything interactive must be reachable with Tab and usable with Enter/Space.
- Focus must be VISIBLE (never remove the outline without a replacement).
- A "skip to content" link as the first focusable element lets keyboard users jump past the navigation.
- In a modal, focus must stay trapped inside until it closes, then return to where it was. The native dialog element does this for free.

Also: every image needs alt text saying what it conveys (empty alt="" for decoration); text contrast at least 4.5:1; never use color as the ONLY signal (add an icon or text); every form input needs a label. Test by tabbing through the page, running Lighthouse, and trying a screen reader.

## Key terms

- Semantic HTML — elements that carry meaning (button, nav, main)
- ARIA — extra attributes for screen readers, used sparingly
- Focus trap — keeping Tab inside an open modal
- Contrast ratio — 4.5:1 minimum for normal text (WCAG AA)

topics.name: Core Web Vitals
topics.description: LCP, CLS, INP and how to measure and improve perceived performance.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

Core Web Vitals are three numbers defined by Google that measure how a page FEELS to a user: how fast the main content appears, how quickly it responds to input, and how much it jumps around while loading. They also influence Google search ranking.

## What problem does it solve?

"The page is slow" is vague. These metrics make user experience measurable, comparable, and fixable — and each one points to a different kind of problem.

## How it works

The three Core metrics:
- LCP (Largest Contentful Paint): how long until the biggest visible element (hero image, headline) appears. Good: ≤ 2.5s. Fixes: preload the hero image, never lazy-load it, compress images (WebP/AVIF), use a CDN, cut render-blocking JavaScript.
- INP (Interaction to Next Paint): how long between a click/keypress and the visible response — measured over the page's whole life. Good: ≤ 200ms. Cause: long JavaScript tasks blocking the main thread. Fixes: break up heavy work, show feedback immediately; in React, useTransition exists exactly for this.
- CLS (Cumulative Layout Shift): how much the layout jumps during loading (you try to tap a button and it moves). Good: ≤ 0.1. Causes: images without width/height, late-loading fonts, ads/banners inserted without reserved space. Fixes: always set image dimensions or aspect-ratio, reserve space with min-height, use font-display: swap.

Supporting metrics, in the order the user experiences them: TTFB (server answers) → FCP (first anything appears) → LCP (main content) → fully interactive.

Measure with Lighthouse and PageSpeed Insights (lab tests) and the web-vitals library / Chrome UX Report (real users). A performance budget — hard limits like "LCP ≤ 2.5s, max 300KB JS" enforced in CI — stops the numbers from slowly rotting.

## Key terms

- LCP / INP / CLS — content speed, responsiveness, visual stability
- Main thread — where JS runs; blocking it freezes interaction
- Layout shift — content moving after it appeared
- Performance budget — hard limits checked on every build

topics.name: JavaScript event loop
topics.description: Call stack, task queue, microtasks, and async execution order.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

The event loop is the mechanism that lets JavaScript — a single-threaded language that can only do one thing at a time — handle timers, network requests and clicks without freezing the page.

## What problem does it solve?

If JavaScript simply waited for every network request to finish, the page would freeze during every fetch. Instead, slow operations are handed to the browser, and their callbacks are queued and run later. The event loop is the scheduler that decides when "later" is.

## How it works

The moving parts:
- Call stack: where the currently running function lives. Synchronous code runs here, one function at a time.
- Web APIs: setTimeout, fetch, DOM events actually run inside the browser, not in your JS. When they finish, they put your callback into a queue.
- Two queues with different priorities: the microtask queue (promise callbacks, queueMicrotask, code after await) and the macrotask queue (setTimeout, setInterval, click events).

The one rule that explains everything: after the current code finishes, the loop runs ALL waiting microtasks before it takes the next macrotask or repaints the screen. That is why in a puzzle mixing console.log, setTimeout(…, 0) and Promise.then, the output order is always: synchronous lines first, then all the promise callbacks, then the timeouts.

Consequences worth knowing:
- setTimeout(fn, 0) is not "immediately" — it means "after all current code and all microtasks".
- async/await is promises in disguise: code before the first await runs synchronously, code after it runs as a microtask.
- A promise that keeps scheduling itself creates an infinite microtask loop that freezes the page completely, because the loop never gets to render.
- For truly parallel CPU work, use a Web Worker — a separate thread with its own event loop that talks to the page via postMessage (no DOM access).

## Key terms

- Call stack — the currently executing functions
- Microtask vs macrotask — promise callbacks always run before timeouts
- Web Worker — a real second thread for heavy computation
- Blocking the main thread — long JS = frozen page

topics.name: Freezing & Locking Objects
topics.description: preventExtensions, seal, freeze — the three lock levels — and the property descriptors underneath.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

Three built-in methods that lock a JavaScript object down, in increasing strictness: Object.preventExtensions (no new properties), Object.seal (no adding or deleting), and Object.freeze (fully read-only).

## What problem does it solve?

JS objects are open by default — anyone can add, change or delete properties at any time. Sometimes you want to protect an object (a config, shared constants) from accidental modification.

## How it works

- preventExtensions: you cannot ADD properties; changing and deleting existing ones still works.
- seal: no adding AND no deleting; changing values still works.
- freeze: nothing can change — read-only. Check with Object.isFrozen() and friends.

Two traps carry the interview points:
- All three are SHALLOW. Object.freeze(settings) does not freeze settings.nested — objects inside can still be modified. A "deep freeze" must walk the object recursively.
- Without strict mode, violations fail SILENTLY — the assignment just does nothing, no error, which makes debugging miserable. In strict mode (and in all ES modules) you get a TypeError. One more reason to always be in strict mode.

Under the hood, these methods flip property descriptors — the three hidden flags every property has: writable (can the value change), configurable (can it be deleted/redefined), enumerable (does it show up in loops). Object.defineProperty lets you set these individually, e.g. a hidden read-only id that never appears in Object.keys.

## Key terms

- preventExtensions / seal / freeze — the three lock levels
- Shallow — nested objects are NOT protected
- Strict mode — makes violations throw instead of silently failing
- Property descriptor — writable / configurable / enumerable flags

topics.name: Generator Functions & Iterators
topics.description: function*, yield, lazy evaluation, infinite sequences, yield* delegation, and the iterator protocol.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

A generator is a function that can PAUSE in the middle and continue later. You declare it with function* and pause it with the yield keyword; each yield hands a value to the caller.

## What problem does it solve?

Normal functions run start-to-finish in one go. Generators let you produce values one at a time, on demand — including infinite sequences — without computing everything up front or holding it all in memory.

## How it works

Calling a generator does NOT run it — it returns an iterator. Each .next() call runs the code until the next yield, then freezes the function with all its local state intact. next() returns { value, done }.

What this enables:
- Lazy sequences: a Fibonacci generator with an infinite loop inside is fine — values are only computed when asked for.
- Two-way communication: next(x) sends a value INTO the generator; it becomes the result of the paused yield.
- Composition: yield* hands control to another iterable (array, another generator) until it is exhausted.
- Practical uses: ID sequences, walking paginated APIs, custom iteration for your own objects.

Generators work with for...of and the spread operator because they implement the iterable protocol: any object with a [Symbol.iterator]() method returning an iterator (something with next()) plugs into those language features — you can write one by hand for e.g. a range object.

Async generators (async function*) combine await and yield, consumed with for await...of — the classic example is streaming results page by page from an API. Trivia with credibility: async/await itself is built on generator machinery.

## Key terms

- function* / yield — declare and pause a generator
- { value, done } — what each next() returns
- Iterable protocol — Symbol.iterator makes for...of work
- Lazy evaluation — compute only when asked
- for await...of — consuming an async generator

topics.name: useTransition, useOptimistic & useLayoutEffect
topics.description: The UX fine-tuning hooks: priority-aware updates, optimistic UI, and pre-paint DOM measurement.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

Three specialized React hooks for fine-tuning user experience: useTransition (keep typing smooth during heavy updates), useOptimistic (show the expected result before the server confirms), and useLayoutEffect (measure the page before it is drawn).

## What problem does it solve?

Each solves one specific UX problem that ordinary state and effects cannot: janky typing, laggy feedback, and visible flicker.

## How it works

useTransition (React 18): you mark a state update as LOW priority. Typing in a search box updates the input immediately (urgent), while filtering a huge list runs in a transition that React can pause and restart if the user keeps typing. The isPending flag lets you show a spinner. The sibling hook useDeferredValue does the same for a value you receive as a prop rather than a setState you own.

useOptimistic (React 19): shows the expected result immediately — the new todo appears in the list the moment you press Enter, marked as pending — and if the server call fails, React rolls the list back automatically. Without it you would hand-code the add-then-remove-on-error dance.

useLayoutEffect: identical to useEffect, but runs BEFORE the browser paints the screen. Use it when you must measure or position something (a tooltip near its target) — the user never sees the un-positioned frame. Because it blocks painting, the default should remain useEffect; switch only when you actually see flicker.

## Key terms

- Transition — an interruptible, low-priority state update
- isPending — "the slow update is still rendering" flag
- Optimistic update — show the expected result, roll back on failure
- Paint — the browser drawing the frame; layout effects run before it

topics.name: useState vs useReducer
topics.description: Choosing between the two state hooks, lazy initialization, and automatic batching.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

React's two hooks for component state: useState for simple independent values, useReducer for complex state where several fields change together according to rules. (Internally, useState is actually built on useReducer.)

## What problem does it solve?

A form with fields, validation errors and a submitting flag juggled across five useState calls becomes hard to follow. useReducer gathers the update logic into one pure function where every change is an explicit, named action.

## How it works

useReducer takes a reducer — a pure function (state, action) → newState — and gives you back the state plus a dispatch function. Instead of calling setters, you dispatch actions: { type: "SET_FIELD", field: "email", value }. All the logic for what each action does lives in the reducer's switch.

Two concrete wins: the reducer is a plain function, so you can unit test the update logic without rendering anything; and updates become traceable — you can log every action, Redux-style.

Rule of thumb: more than 2–3 useState values that change together → switch to useReducer.

Two performance details that go with this topic:
- Lazy initialization: if computing the initial state is expensive (reading localStorage, parsing JSON), pass a FUNCTION to useState: useState(() => JSON.parse(...)). It runs only on the first render. Passing the value directly re-runs the computation on every render just to throw it away.
- Automatic batching (React 18): several setState calls in the same moment — even inside setTimeout or a promise — produce ONE re-render, not several. You rarely need to think about it; flushSync forces an immediate render in the rare case you do.

## Key terms

- Reducer — pure function turning (state, action) into new state
- dispatch — send an action instead of calling a setter
- Lazy initializer — useState(() => expensive()) runs once
- Batching — multiple setStates, one render

topics.name: Imperative Child APIs (refs)
topics.description: forwardRef + useImperativeHandle, the React 19 ref prop, and callback refs.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

The ref mechanism that lets a parent component directly call functions on a child — like focus() or clear() on a custom input — stepping outside React's normal one-way data flow.

## What problem does it solve?

React data flows down through props. But some things are inherently commands, not data: "focus this field", "scroll to top", "play the video". For those, the parent needs a handle on the child.

## How it works

The classic pattern (React ≤ 18): wrap the child in forwardRef so the parent's ref reaches it, and inside the child use useImperativeHandle to decide WHAT the parent gets. Instead of exposing the raw DOM node, you expose a small chosen API — { focus, clear } — so the parent can do exactly what you allow and nothing more.

In React 19, ref became a normal prop, so forwardRef is no longer needed — but useImperativeHandle keeps its role of limiting what is exposed.

The alternative: a callback ref. Instead of a ref object you pass a FUNCTION, which React calls with the DOM node when it appears (and with null when it is removed). Handy when something must happen exactly at mount — measuring the element, attaching an IntersectionObserver.

The discipline that interviewers listen for: refs are a last resort. If the same thing can be done with props and callbacks — and state usually can — do that. Legitimate ref uses are DOM commands: focus, scroll, media, animation, integrating non-React libraries.

## Key terms

- forwardRef — passes the parent's ref into the child (pre-React-19)
- useImperativeHandle — expose a chosen small API, not the raw node
- Callback ref — a function called at mount/unmount with the node
- Escape hatch — refs bypass the normal data flow; use sparingly

topics.name: noopener, noreferrer & Tabnabbing
topics.description: Why target="_blank" is a security hole and what the rel attributes fix.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

Two values of the rel attribute on links (rel="noopener noreferrer") that make target="_blank" links safe.

## What problem does it solve?

A link with target="_blank" opens in a new tab — and historically the opened page received a JavaScript reference to YOUR page: window.opener. A malicious page could use it to redirect your original tab: window.opener.location = "https://fake-bank-login.com". The user returns to your tab, finds what looks like a login page, and types their password. This attack is called tabnabbing.

## How it works

- noopener: the new page gets no window.opener — it cannot touch your tab. Bonus: the browser may run the new page in a separate process, which is also better for performance.
- noreferrer: additionally, the browser does not send the Referer header, so the target site does not learn which page the visitor came from. noreferrer implies noopener, so technically it alone is enough — writing both just makes the intent obvious.

Modern browsers now apply noopener automatically on target="_blank" links, but noreferrer still requires opting in, old browsers exist, and the ESLint rule react/jsx-no-target-blank keeps the habit enforced.

The same hole exists with window.open() from JavaScript — pass "noopener,noreferrer" as its third argument.

Related but different: rel="nofollow" is an SEO instruction telling search engines not to follow the link — used on user-submitted links against spam. It has nothing to do with security.

## Key terms

- Tabnabbing — the opened page redirecting your original tab
- window.opener — the reference that makes the attack possible
- Referer header — tells the target where the visitor came from
- nofollow — SEO-only cousin, not a security feature

topics.name: Redux Toolkit & Selector Performance
topics.description: createSlice, extraReducers, useSelector re-render pitfalls, shallowEqual/createSelector, RTK Query.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

Redux Toolkit (RTK) is the official, modern way to write Redux. It removes the boilerplate that classic Redux was infamous for, and includes solutions for async data loading and re-render performance.

## What problem does it solve?

Classic Redux needed action type constants, action creators and switch-based reducers written by hand for every little thing. RTK's createSlice generates all of that from one definition — and thanks to the built-in Immer library, you can write state.list.push(item) and still get correct immutable updates behind the scenes.

## How it works

A slice declares its state and its reducers; RTK generates the action creators. For async work, createAsyncThunk wraps an API call and emits pending / fulfilled / rejected actions, which you handle in the slice's extraReducers to drive loading/error/data state.

Reading state uses useSelector — and here lives the classic performance bug. useSelector re-renders the component when the selected value changes, compared with ===. If your selector returns a NEW array or object every time — state.users.filter(...) creates a new array on every call — the comparison always says "changed", and the component re-renders on EVERY store update, even when the data is identical.

Three fixes, in order of preference:
- Select primitives separately (a number, a boolean) — === works naturally.
- createSelector: a memoized selector that recomputes only when its inputs change. Best for derived data.
- shallowEqual as useSelector's second argument: compares the object's first level by value instead of by reference.

RTK Query is the built-in data-fetching layer: define endpoints, get generated hooks (useGetUsersQuery) with caching and automatic refetching when a mutation invalidates a tag — replacing hand-written thunk + loading-state code entirely (same idea as React Query).

## Key terms

- createSlice — state + reducers in, action creators out
- Immer — write "mutations", get immutable updates
- Selector returning a new reference — the classic re-render bug
- createSelector / shallowEqual — the two standard fixes
- RTK Query — built-in data fetching with cache invalidation

topics.name: Closures, var/let & the TDZ
topics.description: The classic var + setTimeout loop puzzle, hoisting differences, and the temporal dead zone.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

The most classic JavaScript interview puzzle: a for loop using var with a setTimeout inside — and the three language concepts it tests at once: variable scope, closures, and the event loop.

## What problem does it solve?

Nothing — it CAUSES a problem, and explaining it well proves you understand how JS actually works.

The puzzle: for (var i = 0; i < 6; i++) { setTimeout(() => console.log(i), 1000); } — what prints? Answer: six times 6, all at once, after one second.

## How it works

Three facts combine:
- var is function-scoped, not block-scoped: there is only ONE i variable for the whole loop, not one per iteration.
- A closure captures the VARIABLE itself, not its value at that moment: all six callbacks share that one i.
- setTimeout callbacks run AFTER the current synchronous code finishes (they are macrotasks) — by then the loop is done and i is 6.

The fixes, each proving a different insight:
- let i — block scope creates a fresh i per iteration. Prints 0–5. The modern fix.
- An IIFE (immediately-invoked function) — creates a new scope per iteration, capturing the current value as a parameter.
- setTimeout's third argument — extra arguments are passed to the callback.
- .bind(null, i) — locks in the current value.

The related theory: all declarations are hoisted (moved to the top of their scope), but differently. var is hoisted WITH the value undefined — reading it early gives undefined, no error. let/const are hoisted but uninitialized: from the block start to the declaration line they are in the Temporal Dead Zone (TDZ), and any access — even typeof — throws a ReferenceError. And const forbids reassignment, but a const array can still be pushed to — the reference is frozen, not the contents.

## Key terms

- Function scope vs block scope — var vs let/const
- Closure — a function remembering the variables around it
- Hoisting — declarations move to the top of the scope
- TDZ (Temporal Dead Zone) — let/const unusable before their line

topics.name: Recursive Object Flattening
topics.description: The flatten/unflatten exercise: recursion, typeof traps, and circular-reference safety.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

A classic live-coding exercise: turn a nested object like { a: { b: { c: "1" } }, e: "x" } into a flat one with dot-joined keys: { "a.b.c": "1", "e": "x" }. It tests whether you can think recursively and whether you know JavaScript's type-checking traps.

## What problem does it solve?

Flat key–value shapes are needed for things like config files, form libraries, or CSV export. But the real point is demonstrating a clean recursive approach out loud.

## How it works

The approach to narrate before coding:
- Walk the object's keys.
- Base case: the value is a primitive → write result[prefixedKey] = value.
- Recursive case: the value is an object → call yourself with the prefix extended ("a" → "a.b").

The reverse (unflatten) splits each key on dots and rebuilds the nesting, creating intermediate objects as it walks.

The edge cases are where the points are — mention them proactively:
- typeof null === "object" — JavaScript's most famous wart. Without an explicit null check you recurse into null and crash.
- Arrays are also objects (typeof [] === "object"). Decide consciously: keep arrays as leaf values (Array.isArray check) or flatten them with index keys.
- Circular references (an object containing itself) cause infinite recursion → "Maximum call stack size exceeded". Guard with a Set of already-visited objects.
- Extremely deep nesting can overflow the stack even without cycles; an iterative version with an explicit stack avoids that.

## Key terms

- Base case / recursive case — the two halves of any recursion
- typeof null === "object" — the historic bug you must guard against
- Array.isArray — the reliable array check
- Circular reference — self-containing object; needs a visited-set

topics.name: Frontend Web Security: XSS, CSP & CORS
topics.description: The OWASP frontend essentials: XSS types and defenses, CSRF, Content-Security-Policy, CORS and security headers.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

The four security topics every frontend developer is expected to know: XSS (injected scripts), CSRF (forged requests), CSP (a browser policy that blocks untrusted resources), and CORS (the rules for cross-site requests).

## What problem does it solve?

Web pages run untrusted input (user comments, URL parameters) next to trusted code, and browsers happily send cookies everywhere. These mechanisms exist because attackers exploit exactly that.

## How it works

XSS (Cross-Site Scripting): an attacker gets THEIR JavaScript to run in YOUR users' browsers — for example by posting a comment containing a script tag, which then runs for everyone who views it. Variants: stored (saved in the database), reflected (echoed from the URL), DOM-based (client code inserts user input into the page). Defense: never assign user input to innerHTML — use textContent. React escapes JSX content by default, so {userText} is safe — but dangerouslySetInnerHTML with user input reopens the hole (the name is the warning).

CSRF (Cross-Site Request Forgery): a malicious page makes YOUR browser send a request to a site you are logged into — and the browser attaches your cookies automatically, so the server thinks it is you. Defense: SameSite cookies, CSRF tokens, checking the Origin header.

CSP (Content-Security-Policy): an HTTP header that whitelists where the browser may load scripts, styles and images from. Its superpower against XSS: it can ban inline scripts entirely, so even an injected <script> tag will not run.

CORS: browsers block JavaScript from reading responses from OTHER origins by default (the Same-Origin Policy) — this is a protection, not a bug. The server opts in with headers like Access-Control-Allow-Origin. Note the subtlety: the request may still reach the server; CORS controls whether the browser lets your code read the answer.

Other headers on the checklist: Strict-Transport-Security (force HTTPS), X-Frame-Options (stop your site being embedded in an iframe for clickjacking), X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff.

## Key terms

- XSS — attacker's script in your page; escape output, avoid innerHTML
- CSRF — riding the user's cookies; SameSite + tokens
- CSP — whitelist of allowed resource sources; blocks inline scripts
- Same-Origin Policy / CORS — the default wall and its controlled gate

topics.name: Microfrontend Architecture
topics.description: Splitting the frontend monolith: integration options, Module Federation, communication, and when NOT to.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

Microfrontends apply the microservice idea to the browser: instead of one big frontend app, independent teams build, deploy and own separate pieces (product page, cart, admin), which are stitched together into one site.

## What problem does it solve?

When many teams work in one frontend codebase, they block each other: one release train, merge conflicts, one framework version for everyone. Microfrontends give each team its own deploy pipeline and technology choices.

## How it works

Integration options, weakest to strongest:
- Build-time (npm packages): each part is a package the shell app imports. Simple — but any change requires rebuilding and redeploying the whole shell, which defeats the purpose.
- iframes: perfect isolation, but painful layout, clumsy communication, duplicated downloads, poor UX seams.
- Module Federation (Webpack/Vite) — the current standard: the shell loads modules at RUNTIME from separately deployed apps, over the network. Shared libraries like React are declared as singletons so they are not downloaded twice. Using a remote component feels like a normal lazy import.
- Web Components: each piece registers a custom HTML element; framework-agnostic, with CSS isolation via Shadow DOM.

The two hard problems:
- Communication: keep it minimal — if two microfrontends talk constantly, they should probably be one. Use DOM custom events, a tiny event bus, or the URL as the shared language.
- Consistency: separate teams drift visually, so a shared, versioned design-system package (buttons, inputs, theme) is practically mandatory.

The honest trade-off: independent deploys and team autonomy versus infrastructure complexity, bigger bundles, and harder testing. Worth it for 5+ teams on one product or a gradual framework migration; for a small team, a well-structured monolith is better.

## Key terms

- Shell / host — the container app that composes the pieces
- Module Federation — runtime loading of remotely deployed modules
- Shared singleton — one React instance across all pieces
- Design system — the shared component library preventing UX drift

topics.name: TypeScript Essentials
topics.description: type vs interface, narrowing, generics, utility types, any/unknown/never, and typing React.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

TypeScript is JavaScript plus static types: you annotate what shape data should have, the compiler checks it while you build, and then the types are stripped away — the browser runs plain JavaScript.

## What problem does it solve?

In plain JS, passing the wrong shape (user.nmae, a string where a number belongs) explodes at RUNTIME, often in production. TypeScript catches these at compile time, and makes editors dramatically smarter (autocomplete, safe renames).

## How it works

The essentials interviews ask about:

- type vs interface: two ways to describe object shapes. interface can be extended and merged (you can add fields to an existing declaration); type is more flexible (unions, intersections). For plain object shapes, either works — for unions and helpers, type.
- Unions and narrowing: string | number means "either". Inside an if (typeof x === "string") branch, the compiler KNOWS it is a string — this is narrowing. The discriminated union pattern (objects sharing a kind field you switch on) is the type-safe way to model variants.
- Generics: type parameters for reusable code — ApiResponse<T> works for any payload type, and the compiler tracks which one.
- any vs unknown vs never: any switches type checking OFF — mistakes compile and crash at runtime; avoid it. unknown is the safe version: you must narrow it before use — right for API responses and JSON.parse. never means "no value can ever be here" — used for exhaustiveness checks that make the compiler complain when a switch misses a case.
- Utility types to recognize: Partial, Required, Pick, Omit, Record, ReturnType.

The most important single sentence: types are ERASED at runtime (type erasure). The compiler checks your code against your declarations, but nothing checks the actual data flowing in at runtime — so external input still needs real validation with a library like zod.

## Key terms

- Structural typing — compatibility by shape, not by name
- Narrowing — the compiler refining a union inside a branch
- any vs unknown — off switch vs safe "must check first"
- Type erasure — types vanish at runtime; validate external data

topics.name: Responsive Design & Layout
topics.description: Mobile-first media queries, Flexbox vs Grid, CSS units, responsive images, and container queries.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

Responsive design means one codebase that adapts its layout to every screen size — phone, tablet, desktop — instead of separate mobile and desktop sites.

## What problem does it solve?

Half your users are on phones. Fixed pixel layouts either overflow small screens or waste large ones.

## How it works

The modern methodology is mobile-first: write the base styles for the small screen, then add @media (min-width: 768px) blocks that enhance the layout as space grows. Also respect user preferences: prefers-color-scheme (dark mode) and prefers-reduced-motion.

Flexbox vs Grid — the most-asked question — is one dimension versus two: Flexbox lays things out along ONE axis (a navbar, a row of buttons) and sizes flow from the content. Grid controls rows AND columns together (page layouts, photo galleries) and you define the tracks up front. The one-liner worth memorizing: grid-template-columns: repeat(auto-fit, minmax(250px, 1fr)) — a card grid that adds and removes columns by available space, with zero media queries.

Units: px for borders and tiny fixed details; rem (relative to the root font size) for text and spacing — it scales when the user increases their browser font size, which is an accessibility win; % relative to the parent; vw/vh relative to the viewport. Fluid typography without breakpoints: font-size: clamp(1.5rem, 5vw, 3rem) — never smaller than 1.5rem, never bigger than 3rem, fluid in between.

Responsive images: srcset lets the browser pick the right resolution; the picture element provides modern-format fallbacks (AVIF → WebP → JPEG); loading="lazy" for below-the-fold images.

The modern tool to name-drop: container queries (@container) — a component responds to ITS OWN container's width rather than the whole viewport, so it adapts correctly wherever it is dropped.

## Key terms

- Mobile-first — base styles for small screens, min-width upwards
- Flexbox = 1D, Grid = 2D — the axis rule
- rem — scales with the user's font settings (accessibility)
- clamp(min, fluid, max) — fluid sizing without media queries
- Container query — respond to the parent, not the viewport

questions.title: Object vs Map
questions.prompt: When would you use a Map instead of a plain object in JavaScript, and what are the differences?
questions.answerStructureHint: Key types, key coercion, iteration order and size, and when each fits.

expectedPoints.label: Map keys can be any type
expectedPoints.description: Object keys are coerced to strings/symbols; Map keys can be objects, functions or any value.

expectedPoints.label: Map preserves insertion order and exposes size
expectedPoints.description: Map iterates in insertion order and has a size property; objects need Object.keys and have no direct size.

expectedPoints.label: Knows when to prefer each
expectedPoints.description: Map for frequent add/remove and non-string keys; object for fixed-shape records and JSON.

followUps.prompt: What problem do WeakMap and WeakSet solve that Map and Set do not?

questions.title: Event delegation
questions.prompt: What is event delegation and why is it useful? Explain capturing vs bubbling.
questions.answerStructureHint: Bubbling vs capturing phases, attaching one listener on an ancestor, and the benefits.

expectedPoints.label: Explains bubbling and capturing
expectedPoints.description: Events propagate down (capture) then up (bubble) through ancestors.

expectedPoints.label: One listener on a common ancestor
expectedPoints.description: Attach a single handler on a parent instead of one per child.

expectedPoints.label: Names the benefits
expectedPoints.description: Works for dynamically added elements and reduces memory/listener count.

followUps.prompt: How do stopPropagation and preventDefault differ?

questions.title: WebSocket vs SSE for live UI
questions.prompt: You need live updates in a web app. How would you choose between WebSocket, server-sent events and polling?
questions.answerStructureHint: Direction (one-way vs two-way), frequency, reconnection, and infrastructure fit.

expectedPoints.label: Frames around the requirement
expectedPoints.description: Anchors the choice in direction and update frequency rather than naming a winner.

expectedPoints.label: WebSocket = bidirectional
expectedPoints.description: WebSocket suits two-way, high-frequency communication like chat.

expectedPoints.label: SSE = one-way server push with auto-reconnect
expectedPoints.description: SSE streams server-to-client over HTTP and reconnects automatically; polling is simplest for low frequency.

followUps.prompt: What breaks first when you scale WebSocket connections, and how would you handle reconnection on the client?

questions.title: CSS box-sizing
questions.prompt: What does box-sizing do, and what is the difference between content-box and border-box?
questions.answerStructureHint: How width is computed with padding and border under each value, and why border-box is common.

expectedPoints.label: border-box includes padding and border in width
expectedPoints.description: With border-box the declared width contains content, padding and border.
acceptedSignals: padding and border inside the width

expectedPoints.label: content-box is the default and adds padding/border on top
expectedPoints.description: content-box (default) sizes only the content, so padding and border expand the element.

followUps.prompt: Why do many CSS resets set box-sizing: border-box on everything?

questions.title: Web accessibility basics
questions.prompt: How do you make a web application accessible? Walk me through the key things you check.
questions.answerStructureHint: Semantic HTML first, keyboard and focus, ARIA where needed, contrast and screen-reader testing.

expectedPoints.label: Semantic HTML first
expectedPoints.description: Use native elements (button, nav, label) before reaching for ARIA.

expectedPoints.label: Keyboard and focus management
expectedPoints.description: Everything usable by keyboard, visible focus, sensible tab order.

expectedPoints.label: ARIA where needed and sufficient contrast
expectedPoints.description: ARIA roles/attributes for custom widgets, plus color contrast and screen-reader checks.

followUps.prompt: How would you actually test that a component is accessible?

questions.title: Core Web Vitals
questions.prompt: What are the Core Web Vitals, and how would you improve a page that scores poorly on them?
questions.answerStructureHint: Name LCP/CLS/INP, measure with real data, then targeted fixes per metric.

expectedPoints.label: Names the metrics
expectedPoints.description: LCP (loading), CLS (layout stability), INP/FID (interactivity).

expectedPoints.label: Measures with real data first
expectedPoints.description: Uses field data / Lighthouse / RUM before optimizing.

expectedPoints.label: Targeted fixes per metric
expectedPoints.description: Optimize images/critical path for LCP, reserve space for CLS, break up long tasks for INP.

followUps.prompt: A page jumps around as it loads. Which metric is that, and what are the usual causes?

questions.title: JavaScript event loop
questions.prompt: Explain the JavaScript event loop. What is the difference between a microtask and a macrotask?
questions.answerStructureHint: Single thread + call stack, the task and microtask queues, and their ordering.

expectedPoints.label: Single-threaded with a call stack and queue
expectedPoints.description: JS runs on one thread; the event loop pulls queued callbacks when the stack is empty.

expectedPoints.label: Microtasks drain before the next macrotask
expectedPoints.description: Promise callbacks (microtasks) run fully before the next timer/task (macrotask).

followUps.prompt: Given a console.log, a setTimeout(…, 0) and a Promise.then in the same function, in what order do they run and why?

questions.title: Locking down a JavaScript object
questions.prompt: What options do you have to prevent an object from being modified in JavaScript, and what are the gotchas?

expectedPoints.label: Three lock levels
expectedPoints.description: preventExtensions (no add) < seal (no add/delete) < freeze (read-only).

expectedPoints.label: Shallow — nested objects stay mutable
expectedPoints.description: freeze does not recurse; deep freeze needs recursion.

expectedPoints.label: Silent failure without strict mode
expectedPoints.description: Non-strict code ignores violations quietly; strict mode throws TypeError.

expectedPoints.label: Property descriptors underneath
expectedPoints.description: writable/configurable/enumerable; defineProperty for custom locks.

questions.title: What are generator functions good for?
questions.prompt: Explain how generator functions work and give practical situations where you would use one.

expectedPoints.label: Pause/resume with yield
expectedPoints.description: function* + yield freezes state; next() resumes; returns {value, done}.

expectedPoints.label: Lazy evaluation / infinite sequences
expectedPoints.description: Values computed on demand — infinite sequences with finite memory.

expectedPoints.label: Iterator/iterable protocol
expectedPoints.description: Symbol.iterator makes for...of and spread work; yield* delegates.

expectedPoints.label: Async generators
expectedPoints.description: async function* + for await...of, e.g. paging through an API.

questions.title: Keeping the UI responsive during heavy updates
questions.prompt: A search input filters a huge list and typing feels janky. Which React tools address this, and how do they work?

expectedPoints.label: useTransition for low-priority updates
expectedPoints.description: Urgent input update applies now; heavy filter runs interruptibly; isPending for feedback.

expectedPoints.label: useDeferredValue alternative
expectedPoints.description: Defer a received value instead of wrapping your own setState.

expectedPoints.label: Why: main thread blocking
expectedPoints.description: Long synchronous render blocks paint; splitting priorities restores responsiveness (this is INP).

followUps.prompt: When would you use useLayoutEffect instead of useEffect?

expectedPoints.label: useLayoutEffect timing
expectedPoints.description: Runs after DOM update but before paint — DOM measurement/positioning without flicker; blocks painting.

questions.title: useState or useReducer?
questions.prompt: How do you decide between useState and useReducer, and what performance details around state initialization and updates are worth knowing?

expectedPoints.label: Complexity-based choice
expectedPoints.description: Simple independent values → useState; interrelated state with branching update logic → useReducer.

expectedPoints.label: Reducer is pure and testable
expectedPoints.description: Test the update logic without rendering; actions are traceable.

expectedPoints.label: Lazy initialization
expectedPoints.description: Pass an initializer function so expensive initial state computes once.

expectedPoints.label: Automatic batching
expectedPoints.description: React 18 batches all same-tick updates into one render, even in timeouts/promises; flushSync opts out.

questions.title: Calling a child component's methods
questions.prompt: A parent needs to trigger focus and reset inside a custom input component. How do you expose that, and what are the caveats?

expectedPoints.label: forwardRef + useImperativeHandle
expectedPoints.description: Forward the ref, expose a narrow API ({focus, clear}), not the raw node.

expectedPoints.label: React 19: ref as a prop
expectedPoints.description: No forwardRef needed anymore — ref arrives as a normal prop.

expectedPoints.label: Refs escape the data flow — last resort
expectedPoints.description: Props and callbacks first; refs only for DOM-imperative needs (focus, scroll, animation).

questions.title: The target="_blank" security hole
questions.prompt: Why do we add rel="noopener noreferrer" to external links?

expectedPoints.label: Tabnabbing via window.opener
expectedPoints.description: The opened page can redirect the original tab to a phishing page.

expectedPoints.label: What each attribute does
expectedPoints.description: noopener severs the opener; noreferrer also hides the Referer and implies noopener.

expectedPoints.label: Modern defaults + window.open
expectedPoints.description: Browsers now imply noopener on _blank; window.open needs the flags explicitly; nofollow is SEO, not security.

questions.title: useSelector re-renders too much
questions.prompt: A component using useSelector re-renders on every store change even though its data hasn't changed. What's happening and how do you fix it?

expectedPoints.label: New reference per selector run
expectedPoints.description: filter/map/object literals return fresh references; === sees change every time.

expectedPoints.label: createSelector / shallowEqual / primitives
expectedPoints.description: Memoized selectors for derived data; shallowEqual second arg; or select primitives separately.

expectedPoints.label: Knows the RTK model
expectedPoints.description: createSlice + Immer 'mutation'; extraReducers for thunk lifecycle actions.

expectedPoints.label: RTK Query for data fetching
expectedPoints.description: Generated hooks, cache, tag invalidation replace manual thunks.

questions.title: The var + setTimeout loop puzzle
questions.prompt: for (var i = 0; i < 6; i++) { setTimeout(() => console.log(i), 1000); } — what does this print, why, and how would you fix it?

expectedPoints.label: Correct output: six 6s
expectedPoints.description: All callbacks print 6, together, after ~1s.

expectedPoints.label: Why: scope + closure + event loop
expectedPoints.description: var is function-scoped (one shared i); closures capture the variable; timeouts run after the loop ends.

expectedPoints.label: Fixes
expectedPoints.description: let per-iteration binding; IIFE; setTimeout's third arg; bind.

expectedPoints.label: Hoisting/TDZ bonus
expectedPoints.description: var hoists with undefined; let/const sit in the temporal dead zone until declared.

questions.title: Flattening a nested object
questions.prompt: Walk me through how you would flatten a nested object into dot-notation keys — approach first, then the edge cases you'd guard against.

expectedPoints.label: Recursive approach with base case
expectedPoints.description: Iterate keys; primitives are the base case; objects recurse with a dot-joined prefix.

expectedPoints.label: typeof null trap
expectedPoints.description: typeof null === 'object' — must exclude null explicitly or crash.

expectedPoints.label: Arrays and circular references
expectedPoints.description: Array.isArray decision; a seen-Set against infinite recursion / stack overflow.

questions.title: XSS and how to prevent it
questions.prompt: What is XSS, what forms does it take, and how do you defend a React application against it — including at the HTTP header level?

expectedPoints.label: Three XSS types
expectedPoints.description: Stored (in DB), reflected (in URL/response), DOM-based (client-side sink).

expectedPoints.label: Dangerous sinks and safe defaults
expectedPoints.description: innerHTML/dangerouslySetInnerHTML with user input; React escapes JSX by default; use textContent.

expectedPoints.label: CSP as a second layer
expectedPoints.description: Whitelist sources; block inline scripts (no unsafe-inline; nonce if needed).

expectedPoints.label: Other security headers
expectedPoints.description: HSTS, X-Frame-Options/frame-ancestors, nosniff, hardened cookies.

questions.title: Do we need microfrontends?
questions.prompt: Your organization is considering microfrontends. Explain the integration options and argue when the architecture pays off — and when it does not.

expectedPoints.label: Independent deploy is the point
expectedPoints.description: Team-owned parts, independently built and deployed; tech freedom per team.

expectedPoints.label: Integration options with trade-offs
expectedPoints.description: npm build-time loses independence; iframe isolates but hurts UX; Module Federation loads remotes at runtime with shared singletons.

expectedPoints.label: When NOT to
expectedPoints.description: Small teams / simple apps → monolith; it pays off at 5+ teams or gradual migration.

expectedPoints.label: Design system + minimal communication
expectedPoints.description: Shared component library against UX drift; custom events/URL for loose coupling.

questions.title: any vs unknown, type vs interface
questions.prompt: Explain the difference between any and unknown, and between type and interface. What does 'type erasure' mean in practice?

expectedPoints.label: any disables, unknown requires narrowing
expectedPoints.description: any turns checking off (runtime crashes compile fine); unknown must be narrowed first — the safe choice for external input.

expectedPoints.label: type vs interface
expectedPoints.description: interface: extends + declaration merging; type: unions/intersections. Either for object shapes.

expectedPoints.label: Type erasure
expectedPoints.description: Types exist only at compile time; runtime validation needs zod or similar.

expectedPoints.label: never + exhaustiveness
expectedPoints.description: never for impossible values; assertNever catches unhandled union cases.

questions.title: Flexbox or Grid — and what makes a layout responsive?
questions.prompt: How do you decide between Flexbox and Grid, and what techniques make a modern layout responsive without a pile of media queries?

expectedPoints.label: Flexbox 1D vs Grid 2D
expectedPoints.description: Flexbox: one axis, content-first (navbars, rows); Grid: two axes, layout-first (page layouts).

expectedPoints.label: Mobile-first with min-width
expectedPoints.description: Base styles are mobile; min-width queries enhance upward.

expectedPoints.label: Fluid techniques without media queries
expectedPoints.description: auto-fit/minmax grids, clamp() typography, min()/max() containers.

expectedPoints.label: rem for a11y + container queries
expectedPoints.description: rem scales with user font settings; @container responds to the parent, not the viewport.
